regulatoryStatus: FDA-approved prescription drug (HIV-associated lipodystrophy; reduction of excess abdominal fat in HIV-infected adults).
mechanismSummary: Synthetic growth hormone–releasing hormone (GHRH) analog that stimulates pituitary growth hormone release and increases IGF-1; used to reduce visceral adipose tissue in HIV lipodystrophy.
benefits: reduction of excess abdominal (visceral) fat in HIV-associated lipodystrophy (on-label)
pairsWellWith: diet and exercise program (standard of care)
avoidWith: off-label use for weight loss (not indicated)
vialCompatibility: Use only product-specific diluent and follow prescribing information; do not blend with other drugs/peptides in the same vial unless a licensed clinician/pharmacist confirms compatibility.
compatibleBlends: Bacteriostatic Water for Injection (USP) — manufacturer-supplied diluent (product-specific).
recommendedDosage: 1.28 mg subcutaneous once daily (EGRIFTA WR). Note: EGRIFTA WR and EGRIFTA SV are not substitutable and have different dosing/reconstitution instructions.
preferredTimeOfDay: Data not currently available (label does not require a specific time); take consistently as directed by prescriber.
weeklyDosageSchedule: Week 1+: 1.28 mg once daily (clinician-directed duration; no validated 'cycle' strategy in labeling).
drugInteractions: Potential interactions with cytochrome P450–metabolized drugs (monitor per prescribing information).
Glucocorticoids: patients on glucocorticoid replacement may require dose adjustments (product label describes potential effects via 11βHSD-1).
optimizationProtein: 0.7–1.0 g/lb/day (≈1.6–2.2 g/kg/day) for active/resistance training; RDA baseline is 0.36 g/lb/day (0.8 g/kg/day).
optimizationCarbs: 1.4–2.3 g/lb/day (≈3–5 g/kg/day) for light–moderate training; up to ~3.2 g/lb/day (≈7 g/kg/day) for higher-volume training; adjust to energy needs.
optimizationExercise: 150–300 min/week moderate activity (or 75–150 min vigorous) + ≥2 strength-training days; daily walking often benefits around ~7,000–10,000 steps (individualized).

regulatoryStatus: Pharmaceutical peptide used/approved in multiple countries for specific indications; not FDA-approved as a drug in the U.S. (availability and approved uses vary by jurisdiction).
mechanismSummary: Immunomodulatory peptide derived from thymic peptides; reported to influence innate and adaptive immune responses (e.g., T-cell and dendritic cell functions, cytokine signaling).
benefits: immune modulation in specific clinical contexts (indication-dependent; evidence varies by disease and study design)
pairsWellWith: standard-of-care therapy for the underlying condition (e.g., antivirals/oncology regimens when studied)
vialCompatibility: Data not currently available; multi-compound vial blending is not supported by widely accepted stability/compatibility data.
recommendedDosage: Data not currently available (dosing is product- and indication-specific, and varies by country/label).
drugInteractions: Data not currently available
optimizationProtein: 0.7–1.0 g/lb/day (≈1.6–2.2 g/kg/day) for active/resistance training; RDA baseline is 0.36 g/lb/day (0.8 g/kg/day).
optimizationCarbs: 1.4–2.3 g/lb/day (≈3–5 g/kg/day) for light–moderate training; up to ~3.2 g/lb/day (≈7 g/kg/day) for higher-volume training; adjust to energy needs.
optimizationExercise: 150–300 min/week moderate activity (or 75–150 min vigorous) + ≥2 strength-training days; daily walking often benefits around ~7,000–10,000 steps (individualized).

regulatoryStatus: Unapproved substance; not FDA-approved for human use. Listed as prohibited under WADA Prohibited List category S0 (Unapproved Substances).
mechanismSummary: Experimental peptide studied mainly in animal and in‑vitro models; proposed tissue-repair effects involve angiogenesis, nitric oxide–related pathways, and other signaling mechanisms (clinical relevance unproven).
benefits: preclinical signals for tissue repair (animal/in‑vitro); human clinical efficacy is not established
vialCompatibility: Not recommended; there is no validated clinical stability/compatibility data and substantial quality/sterility concerns exist for non-approved injectable sources.
recommendedDosage: Data not currently available (no established clinical dosing).
drugInteractions: Data not currently available
optimizationProtein: 0.7–1.0 g/lb/day (≈1.6–2.2 g/kg/day) for active/resistance training; RDA baseline is 0.36 g/lb/day (0.8 g/kg/day).
optimizationCarbs: 1.4–2.3 g/lb/day (≈3–5 g/kg/day) for light–moderate training; up to ~3.2 g/lb/day (≈7 g/kg/day) for higher-volume training; adjust to energy needs.
optimizationExercise: 150–300 min/week moderate activity (or 75–150 min vigorous) + ≥2 strength-training days; daily walking often benefits around ~7,000–10,000 steps (individualized).

regulatoryStatus: Unapproved substance; not FDA-approved for human use. Listed as prohibited under WADA Prohibited List category S0 (Unapproved Substances).
mechanismSummary: Synthetic fragment derived from Thymosin Beta-4; preclinical work suggests effects on actin-binding, cell migration, angiogenesis, and tissue-repair signaling. Human clinical efficacy is not established.
benefits: preclinical signals for tissue repair and cell migration (animal/in‑vitro); human clinical efficacy is not established
vialCompatibility: Not recommended; there is no validated clinical stability/compatibility data and substantial quality/sterility concerns exist for non-approved injectable sources.
recommendedDosage: Data not currently available (no established clinical dosing).
drugInteractions: Data not currently available
optimizationProtein: 0.7–1.0 g/lb/day (≈1.6–2.2 g/kg/day) for active/resistance training; RDA baseline is 0.36 g/lb/day (0.8 g/kg/day).
optimizationCarbs: 1.4–2.3 g/lb/day (≈3–5 g/kg/day) for light–moderate training; up to ~3.2 g/lb/day (≈7 g/kg/day) for higher-volume training; adjust to energy needs.
optimizationExercise: 150–300 min/week moderate activity (or 75–150 min vigorous) + ≥2 strength-training days; daily walking often benefits around ~7,000–10,000 steps (individualized).

regulatoryStatus: Cosmetic ingredient in topical products; not FDA-approved for systemic/injectable human use.
mechanismSummary: Naturally occurring copper-binding tripeptide; preclinical and topical literature describe wound-healing, anti-inflammatory, and extracellular-matrix-remodeling signals. Systemic clinical efficacy is not established.
benefits: preclinical and topical signals for wound healing and skin/connective-tissue support; systemic clinical efficacy is not established
avoidWith: known copper-handling disorders (e.g., Wilson's disease) without clinician oversight
vialCompatibility: Not recommended for self-prepared injectable use; topical formulations have established cosmetic stability, but injectable stability/compatibility data are not validated.
recommendedDosage: Data not currently available (no established clinical dosing for systemic use).
drugInteractions: Data not currently available
optimizationProtein: 0.7–1.0 g/lb/day (≈1.6–2.2 g/kg/day) for active/resistance training; RDA baseline is 0.36 g/lb/day (0.8 g/kg/day).
optimizationCarbs: 1.4–2.3 g/lb/day (≈3–5 g/kg/day) for light–moderate training; up to ~3.2 g/lb/day (≈7 g/kg/day) for higher-volume training; adjust to energy needs.
optimizationExercise: 150–300 min/week moderate activity (or 75–150 min vigorous) + ≥2 strength-training days; daily walking often benefits around ~7,000–10,000 steps (individualized).

mechanismSummary: Increases intramuscular creatine/phosphocreatine stores, supporting rapid ATP resynthesis during short, high-intensity efforts and promoting training adaptations (strength/lean mass).
benefits: strength and power performance
lean mass gains with resistance training
high training-load tolerance (evidence varies by context)
pairsWellWith: carbohydrate or carbohydrate+protein co-ingestion may increase uptake (not required)
recommendedDosage: Maintenance: 3–5 g/day. Optional loading: ~0.3 g/kg/day for 5–7 days, then 3–5 g/day to maintain elevated stores.
preferredTimeOfDay: Any time of day (timing not critical; build daily habit).
weeklyDosageSchedule: Option A (loading): Days 1–7 (~Week 1): ~0.3 g/kg/day split doses; Week 2+: 3–5 g/day.
Option B (no loading): Weeks 1–4: 3–5 g/day (stores rise over ~3–4 weeks).
drugInteractions: Data not currently available (no well-established clinically significant interactions in healthy individuals); use clinician oversight with renal disease or nephrotoxic medications.
optimizationProtein: 0.7–1.0 g/lb/day (≈1.6–2.2 g/kg/day) for active/resistance training; RDA baseline is 0.36 g/lb/day (0.8 g/kg/day).
optimizationCarbs: 1.4–2.3 g/lb/day (≈3–5 g/kg/day) for light–moderate training; up to ~3.2 g/lb/day (≈7 g/kg/day) for higher-volume training; adjust to energy needs.
optimizationSupplements: Electrolytes and hydration when training hard
optimizationExercise: Progressive resistance training 2–4×/week + aerobic base per guidelines; daily steps as above.

regulatoryStatus: Dietary supplement; prescription omega‑3 products exist and have different labeling/dosing.
mechanismSummary: Long-chain omega‑3 fatty acids that lower triglycerides (dose-dependent) and influence inflammatory mediators; high doses can affect platelet aggregation and bleeding time.
benefits: triglyceride reduction (dose-dependent)
cardiometabolic support in select contexts (evidence depends on product/formulation and population)
pairsWellWith: statin therapy when clinically indicated (adjunct; physician-directed)
avoidWith: high-dose use with anticoagulants without clinician monitoring
high-dose (e.g., 4 g/day) use in patients with atrial fibrillation risk (caution; evidence suggests slight AF risk increase in some trials)
recommendedDosage: Common supplement range: ~1–2 g/day combined EPA+DHA. Higher doses (e.g., 2–4 g/day) are often used for triglycerides, typically via prescription products. FDA notes ≤5 g/day EPA+DHA from dietary supplements is safe when used as recommended.
preferredTimeOfDay: With meals (often divided) to improve tolerability/absorption.
weeklyDosageSchedule: Week 1+: daily EPA/DHA intake per target dose; reassess lipids after ~8–12 weeks if used for triglycerides.
drugInteractions: Warfarin and similar anticoagulants: possible INR/clotting-time effects; monitoring is advised, especially at higher doses.
Antiplatelet effects at high doses may increase bleeding time in some contexts.
optimizationProtein: 0.7–1.0 g/lb/day (≈1.6–2.2 g/kg/day) for active/resistance training; RDA baseline is 0.36 g/lb/day (0.8 g/kg/day).
optimizationCarbs: 1.4–2.3 g/lb/day (≈3–5 g/kg/day) for light–moderate training; up to ~3.2 g/lb/day (≈7 g/kg/day) for higher-volume training; adjust to energy needs.
optimizationExercise: 150–300 min/week moderate activity (or 75–150 min vigorous) + ≥2 strength-training days; daily walking often benefits around ~7,000–10,000 steps (individualized).

regulatoryStatus: Dietary supplement; also available in prescription products/formulations.
mechanismSummary: Fat-soluble vitamin; converted to 25(OH)D and then active 1,25(OH)2D (calcitriol). Supports calcium/phosphate homeostasis and bone mineralization.
benefits: corrects vitamin D inadequacy/deficiency
supports bone health (rickets/osteomalacia prevention)
avoidWith: excessive dosing that can cause hypercalcemia/hypercalciuria
thiazide diuretics without medical monitoring (hypercalcemia risk)
high-dose vitamin D plus high-dose calcium without clinical indication/monitoring
recommendedDosage: Adults: RDA typically 600 IU/day (19–70 years) and 800 IU/day (>70). Adult UL is generally 4,000 IU/day. Deficiency treatment dosing is clinician-directed.
preferredTimeOfDay: Any time; often taken with food for adherence.
weeklyDosageSchedule: Week 1+: daily intake near RDA unless clinician directs otherwise; recheck 25(OH)D if clinically indicated.
drugInteractions: Orlistat can reduce absorption of vitamin D.
Corticosteroid medications can reduce calcium absorption and impair vitamin D metabolism.
Thiazide diuretics + vitamin D can increase hypercalcemia risk in susceptible patients.
High vitamin D intakes may interact with some statins via shared metabolizing enzymes (evidence mixed).
optimizationProtein: 0.7–1.0 g/lb/day (≈1.6–2.2 g/kg/day) for active/resistance training; RDA baseline is 0.36 g/lb/day (0.8 g/kg/day).
optimizationCarbs: 1.4–2.3 g/lb/day (≈3–5 g/kg/day) for light–moderate training; up to ~3.2 g/lb/day (≈7 g/kg/day) for higher-volume training; adjust to energy needs.
optimizationSupplements: Calcium primarily from food unless clinician recommends supplements
optimizationExercise: 150–300 min/week moderate activity (or 75–150 min vigorous) + ≥2 strength-training days; daily walking often benefits around ~7,000–10,000 steps (individualized).

regulatoryStatus: Dietary supplement; also present in antacids/laxatives (medication doses differ).
mechanismSummary: Essential mineral and cofactor in >300 enzyme systems; roles in neuromuscular function, energy metabolism, blood glucose control, and blood pressure regulation.
benefits: corrects magnesium deficiency/insufficiency
supports muscle/nerve function
migraine prevention support in some guidelines (dose often exceeds supplement UL; clinician supervision)
pairsWellWith: dietary pattern rich in magnesium (leafy greens, legumes, nuts, whole grains)
avoidWith: high-dose supplemental magnesium causing diarrhea and GI symptoms
recommendedDosage: Adults: RDA roughly 400–420 mg/day (men) and 310–320 mg/day (women) from all sources; supplemental UL is 350 mg/day for adults (excludes food magnesium).
preferredTimeOfDay: Any time (often evening for adherence; timing is preference-based).
weeklyDosageSchedule: Week 1+: daily magnesium toward RDA from diet; supplement only as needed based on intake/tolerance.
drugInteractions: Oral bisphosphonates: magnesium can decrease absorption (separate dosing).
Tetracycline and quinolone antibiotics: magnesium can chelate and reduce absorption (separate dosing).
Diuretics and proton pump inhibitors can affect magnesium status; monitor if long-term.
optimizationProtein: 0.7–1.0 g/lb/day (≈1.6–2.2 g/kg/day) for active/resistance training; RDA baseline is 0.36 g/lb/day (0.8 g/kg/day).
optimizationCarbs: 1.4–2.3 g/lb/day (≈3–5 g/kg/day) for light–moderate training; up to ~3.2 g/lb/day (≈7 g/kg/day) for higher-volume training; adjust to energy needs.
optimizationExercise: 150–300 min/week moderate activity (or 75–150 min vigorous) + ≥2 strength-training days; daily walking often benefits around ~7,000–10,000 steps (individualized).

regulatoryStatus: Dietary supplement (OTC); not FDA-approved as a drug.
mechanismSummary: Endogenous cofactor in mitochondrial electron transport and cellular energy production; antioxidant. Supplementation studied in cardiovascular and metabolic conditions.
benefits: adjunct support in heart failure and other cardiovascular contexts (evidence varies)
possible support for statin-associated muscle symptoms (evidence mixed)
avoidWith: warfarin without closer INR monitoring (possible decreased anticoagulant effect)
blood pressure medications without monitoring in hypotension-prone individuals (possible additive BP lowering; magnitude variable)
recommendedDosage: Common studied range: ~100–300 mg/day (often divided; product form and indication vary).
preferredTimeOfDay: With meals (often split doses) to improve absorption/tolerability.
weeklyDosageSchedule: Week 1+: daily dosing in the 100–300 mg/day range; evaluate effect over ~8–12 weeks for symptom-based goals.
drugInteractions: Warfarin: reports of reduced anticoagulant effect/INR changes; monitor INR if combined.
Potential additive blood pressure lowering with antihypertensive drugs (evidence mixed).
optimizationProtein: 0.7–1.0 g/lb/day (≈1.6–2.2 g/kg/day) for active/resistance training; RDA baseline is 0.36 g/lb/day (0.8 g/kg/day).
optimizationCarbs: 1.4–2.3 g/lb/day (≈3–5 g/kg/day) for light–moderate training; up to ~3.2 g/lb/day (≈7 g/kg/day) for higher-volume training; adjust to energy needs.
optimizationExercise: 150–300 min/week moderate activity (or 75–150 min vigorous) + ≥2 strength-training days; daily walking often benefits around ~7,000–10,000 steps (individualized).

regulatoryStatus: Marketed as a dietary supplement ingredient; FDA has issued a GRAS 'no questions' response for certain food uses of nicotinamide riboside chloride.
mechanismSummary: Vitamin B3 derivative and NAD+ precursor; increases NAD+ metabolites in humans, potentially influencing redox balance and cellular energy pathways.
benefits: increases blood biomarkers of NAD+ metabolism in humans
clinical endpoint benefits remain inconsistent across trials and populations (under investigation)
pairsWellWith: exercise and dietary interventions (standard metabolic health foundations)
recommendedDosage: Common studied range ~300–1,000 mg/day; some trials use higher doses. Long-term clinical outcome data remain limited.
weeklyDosageSchedule: Week 1+: daily dosing within studied range; reassess tolerance and clinical goals periodically.
drugInteractions: Data not currently available (no well-characterized interactions in the clinical literature).
optimizationProtein: 0.7–1.0 g/lb/day (≈1.6–2.2 g/kg/day) for active/resistance training; RDA baseline is 0.36 g/lb/day (0.8 g/kg/day).
optimizationCarbs: 1.4–2.3 g/lb/day (≈3–5 g/kg/day) for light–moderate training; up to ~3.2 g/lb/day (≈7 g/kg/day) for higher-volume training; adjust to energy needs.
optimizationExercise: 150–300 min/week moderate activity (or 75–150 min vigorous) + ≥2 strength-training days; daily walking often benefits around ~7,000–10,000 steps (individualized).

regulatoryStatus: FDA-approved prescription drug for type 2 diabetes; historically common first-line therapy in guidelines.
mechanismSummary: Lowers glucose primarily by reducing hepatic glucose production and acting on the gut; multiple molecular mechanisms (including AMPK-related and other pathways) contribute.
benefits: improves glycemic control in type 2 diabetes
weight-neutral to modest weight loss
low hypoglycemia risk as monotherapy
pairsWellWith: other clinician-directed diabetes therapies (e.g., GLP-1 RA or SGLT2 inhibitor)
avoidWith: temporary discontinuation around iodinated contrast imaging in at-risk patients (per label)
recommendedDosage: ER example: start 500 mg once daily with evening meal; increase by 500 mg weekly based on glycemic control/tolerability to max 2,000 mg once daily; if needed, 1,000 mg twice daily (clinician-directed).
preferredTimeOfDay: With meals; ER formulations commonly taken with the evening meal to reduce GI side effects (per labeling).
weeklyDosageSchedule: Week 1: 500 mg once daily with evening meal.
Week 2: 1,000 mg once daily (if tolerated/needed).
Week 3: 1,500 mg once daily (if tolerated/needed).
Week 4+: up to 2,000 mg once daily (maximum labeled ER dose); clinician-directed adjustments thereafter.
drugInteractions: Iodinated contrast procedures: temporarily discontinue in specified risk groups and reassess renal function before restarting (per label).
Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (e.g., topiramate): may increase lactic acidosis risk; consider more frequent monitoring.
Cationic drugs that reduce renal clearance (e.g., cimetidine): may increase metformin exposure and lactic acidosis risk.
Insulin/sulfonylureas: increased hypoglycemia risk—may require dose reduction of the other agent.
Excess alcohol intake potentiates metformin effects on lactate metabolism and increases lactic acidosis risk.
optimizationProtein: 0.7–1.0 g/lb/day (≈1.6–2.2 g/kg/day) for active/resistance training; RDA baseline is 0.36 g/lb/day (0.8 g/kg/day).
optimizationCarbs: If optimizing glycemic control: distribute carbohydrates across meals; total grams depend on energy needs and diabetes care plan (individualized).
optimizationSupplements: Vitamin B12 status monitoring (relevant for long-term use)
optimizationExercise: 150–300 min/week moderate activity (or 75–150 min vigorous) + ≥2 strength-training days; daily walking often benefits around ~7,000–10,000 steps (individualized).

regulatoryStatus: FDA-approved prescription drug for chronic weight management; label also includes cardiovascular risk reduction in adults with established CVD and overweight/obesity and an accelerated approval indication for noncirrhotic MASH with moderate-to-advanced fibrosis (label-specific).
mechanismSummary: GLP-1 receptor agonist that reduces appetite and energy intake and can slow gastric emptying; improves cardiometabolic risk factors and reduces cardiovascular events in select populations.
benefits: clinically meaningful weight loss
cardiovascular event risk reduction in select patients with established CVD and overweight/obesity
metabolic improvements (e.g., glycemia and BP)
pairsWellWith: resistance training + higher protein intake to preserve lean mass during weight loss
avoidWith: personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2 (contraindication)
vialCompatibility: Do not mix with other drugs; supplied as single-dose pen. No manufacturer-supported multi-drug vial blending.
recommendedDosage: Initiation/escalation: 0.25 mg weekly (weeks 1–4), 0.5 mg (weeks 5–8), 1 mg (weeks 9–12), 1.7 mg (weeks 13–16); maintenance from week 17 onward is 2.4 mg weekly (recommended) or 1.7 mg (tolerability-based; indication-dependent).
preferredTimeOfDay: Same day each week, any time of day, with or without meals (per label).
weeklyDosageSchedule: Weeks 1–4: 0.25 mg once weekly.
Weeks 5–8: 0.5 mg once weekly.
Weeks 9–12: 1 mg once weekly.
Weeks 13–16: 1.7 mg once weekly.
Week 17+: 2.4 mg once weekly (or 1.7 mg if not tolerated; indication-dependent).
drugInteractions: Insulin or insulin secretagogues (e.g., sulfonylureas): increased hypoglycemia risk; may require dose reduction of concomitant agent.
Delayed gastric emptying may influence absorption of some oral medications; monitor narrow-therapeutic-index drugs.
optimizationProtein: 0.8–1.0 g/lb/day is often prioritized during pharmacologic weight loss to preserve lean mass (within the 1.6–2.2 g/kg/day evidence-based range for trained individuals).
optimizationCarbs: Adjust to energy deficit and training needs; prioritize fiber/low-GI carbs if glycemia is a concern.
optimizationSupplements: Electrolytes and fiber strategies for GI tolerability
optimizationExercise: Resistance training 2–4×/week + aerobic activity per guidelines; increased daily walking to support energy expenditure and cardiometabolic health.

regulatoryStatus: FDA-approved prescription drug for chronic weight management; label also includes an indication for obstructive sleep apnea in adults with obesity (label-specific).
mechanismSummary: Dual GIP and GLP-1 receptor agonist that reduces appetite and energy intake and improves metabolic regulation; produces large sustained weight loss in trials.
benefits: substantial weight loss
improvements in cardiometabolic risk factors
reduction in obstructive sleep apnea severity in obesity (trial evidence; indication dependent)
pairsWellWith: PAP therapy when clinically indicated for OSA
avoidWith: personal/family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2 (contraindication)
vialCompatibility: Do not mix with other drugs/peptides; no manufacturer-supported multi-drug vial blending guidance.
recommendedDosage: Start 2.5 mg weekly for 4 weeks (not a maintenance dose). After 4 weeks, increase to 5 mg weekly. Dose may be increased in 2.5 mg increments after at least 4 weeks on the current dose until the recommended maintenance dose is achieved. Maintenance: 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg weekly; maximum 15 mg weekly.
preferredTimeOfDay: Once weekly at any time of day, with or without meals (per label).
weeklyDosageSchedule: Weeks 1–4: 2.5 mg once weekly (initiation only).
Weeks 5–8: 5 mg once weekly.
Weeks 9–12: 7.5 mg once weekly (if additional escalation needed).
Weeks 13–16: 10 mg once weekly.
Weeks 17–20: 12.5 mg once weekly.
Week 21+: 15 mg once weekly (max; maintenance as tolerated/needed).
drugInteractions: Insulin or insulin secretagogues (e.g., sulfonylureas): increased hypoglycemia risk; may require dose reduction of concomitant agent.
Delayed gastric emptying can impact absorption of oral drugs; label includes contraception-related counseling for oral contraceptives (timing/backup method) during initiation and dose escalation.
optimizationProtein: 0.8–1.0 g/lb/day is often prioritized during pharmacologic weight loss to preserve lean mass (within the 1.6–2.2 g/kg/day evidence-based range for trained individuals).
optimizationCarbs: Adjust to energy deficit and training needs; prioritize fiber/low-GI carbs if glycemia is a concern.
optimizationSupplements: Electrolytes and fiber strategies for GI tolerability
optimizationExercise: Resistance training 2–4×/week + aerobic activity per guidelines; increased daily walking to support energy expenditure and cardiometabolic health.

regulatoryStatus: FDA-approved prescription hormone replacement for men with low testosterone due to specific medical conditions. Injectable testosterone cypionate is a Schedule III controlled substance.
mechanismSummary: Androgen replacement restores physiologic testosterone levels; acts via androgen receptor and downstream gene transcription; tissue conversion to dihydrotestosterone contributes to effects.
benefits: improves symptoms of testosterone deficiency in appropriately diagnosed men
supports sexual function, secondary sex characteristics, bone density and body composition (context dependent)
pairsWellWith: clinician monitoring (testosterone level, hematocrit, PSA as indicated)
avoidWith: use for athletic performance enhancement (not indicated)
men actively trying to conceive (fertility suppression risk)
vialCompatibility: Do not mix with other drugs in the same vial/syringe unless directed by a clinician; compatibility varies by product and is generally not established.
recommendedDosage: Varies by formulation. Example (testosterone cypionate label): 50–400 mg intramuscularly every 2–4 weeks for replacement in hypogonadal males. Topical gels commonly start around 50 mg daily (product-specific).
frequency: Product-dependent: injections every 2–4 weeks (label example) or topical daily; individualized by clinician.
preferredTimeOfDay: Topical gels are often applied in the morning; injection schedules are clinician-directed (product-specific).
weeklyDosageSchedule: Data not currently available (no universal week-by-week titration; dosing is individualized and product-dependent).
drugInteractions: May increase sensitivity to oral anticoagulants (dose adjustment/monitoring may be needed).
In diabetic patients, androgen therapy may reduce blood glucose and insulin requirements.
FDA requires labeling to include blood pressure increase warnings class-wide (monitor BP).
optimizationProtein: 0.7–1.0 g/lb/day (≈1.6–2.2 g/kg/day) for active/resistance training; RDA baseline is 0.36 g/lb/day (0.8 g/kg/day).
optimizationCarbs: 1.4–2.3 g/lb/day (≈3–5 g/kg/day) for light–moderate training; up to ~3.2 g/lb/day (≈7 g/kg/day) for higher-volume training; adjust to energy needs.
optimizationSleep: ≥7 hours/night; prioritize consistent sleep timing because sleep restriction can reduce testosterone levels.
optimizationExercise: Resistance training 2–4×/week plus aerobic work per guidelines; avoid extreme overtraining without recovery.

regulatoryStatus: Dietary supplement in the U.S.; prescription-only in some countries. Evidence supports short-term use for some circadian rhythm sleep disorders; major guidelines do not recommend it as routine therapy for chronic insomnia in adults due to limited/low-quality evidence.
mechanismSummary: Hormone involved in circadian rhythm regulation; exogenous melatonin can shift circadian phase depending on dose and timing and may modestly reduce sleep-onset latency.
benefits: circadian phase shifting in delayed sleep-wake phase disorder
modest improvement in sleep onset in some settings (effect size varies)
pairsWellWith: sleep timing interventions and bright-light therapy for circadian disorders
avoidWith: anticoagulants or antiplatelet drugs without clinician monitoring (possible bleeding interaction)
blood pressure medications in patients with difficult-to-control hypertension (caution)
recommendedDosage: Doses studied for insomnia/circadian indications vary; a broad evidence base includes ~0.5–6 mg. Timing is indication-specific; chronic insomnia guidelines were based on trials around 2 mg.
preferredTimeOfDay: Evening; timing depends on goal (sleep onset vs circadian phase shifting).
weeklyDosageSchedule: Data not currently available (no universally validated week-by-week titration/cycling strategy).
drugInteractions: Potential interaction with anticoagulants/antiplatelets (bleeding risk) and possible interaction with blood pressure medications; consult clinician/pharmacist before combining.
optimizationProtein: 0.7–1.0 g/lb/day (≈1.6–2.2 g/kg/day) for active/resistance training; RDA baseline is 0.36 g/lb/day (0.8 g/kg/day).
optimizationCarbs: 1.4–2.3 g/lb/day (≈3–5 g/kg/day) for light–moderate training; up to ~3.2 g/lb/day (≈7 g/kg/day) for higher-volume training; adjust to energy needs.
optimizationSupplements: Avoid stacking sedatives without medical guidance
optimizationSleep: Target 7–9 hours; maintain consistent sleep/wake timing and morning light exposure.
optimizationExercise: 150–300 min/week moderate activity (or 75–150 min vigorous) + ≥2 strength-training days; daily walking often benefits around ~7,000–10,000 steps (individualized).
